text: The patient is positioned supine, and after administering preoperative anesthesia, the surgeon begins dissection to retract the gallbladder. The hepatic flexure of the colon is mobilized, separating the colon from the superior surface of the duodenum and extending dissection to the appendix. The ascending colon is fully separated, revealing the duodenum. The surgeon then separates the duodenum from lateral adhesions, encountering significant adhesions likely due to a retroperitoneal mass. A slipped double pigtail stent is identified within the common bile duct. The duodenum is denuded, and caucarization begins from the superior end, progressing towards the second and third parts of the duodenum.

The patient is positioned with the right side elevated and head slightly lowered. The surgeon retracts the duodenum medially, using an energy device to separate lateral adhesions. The inferior vena cava (IVC) becomes visible as the duodenum is retracted. The surgeon identifies knotty adhesions in the area, which are otherwise flimsy and easily separable. The retroperitoneal mass lesion is visible behind the third part of the duodenum.

The surgeon identifies a conglomerated lymph nodal mass adjacent to the aorta and IVC. The dissection begins at the lateral edge to reveal the IVC, which is densely adherent to the mass. Fibro-fatty tissue over the mass is separated, and due to the adhesion, a biopsy is performed using sharp scissors, taking two large tissue samples.

The surgeon uses scissors to take a biopsy from the mass, avoiding tissue charring. The biopsy specimen is placed into a retrieval bag to prevent loss in the abdominal cavity. A gauze piece is applied for hemostatic effect, followed by bipolar cautery to cauterize the surface of the mass near the dissected IVC and aorta. Ab gel strips are applied for additional hemostasis.
The surgeon repositions the duodenum back to its original location, using it as a tamponade to aid in hemostasis. A surgical drain is placed following the completion of the cholecystectomy.
